skill.name: orchestrating-parallel-agents
skill.description: Coordinate multiple AI coding agents on related GitHub issues using git worktrees, staged execution waves, and manual review checkpoints.
skill.summary: Plan and monitor parallel coding agents across GitHub issues and git worktrees.
skill.author: 4eyedengineer

security_audit.summary: Static detections for weak cryptography and network reconnaissance are false positives based on the reviewed Markdown content. The skill is not executable malware, but it instructs users to pre-approve Bash commands, spawn concurrent agents, and run git or GitHub CLI commands that can modify remote repositories.

medium_findings.title: Broad Shell Permission Pre-Approval Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill tells users to pre-approve Bash git add, commit, and push permissions for non-interactive agent runs. This is legitimate workflow guidance, but it increases impact if an agent acts outside the intended scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission examples are explicit and semantically tied to non-interactive execution. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the commands target user-selected worktrees.

medium_findings.title: Parallel Agents Can Modify Remote Repositories
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes spawning concurrent agents, committing changes, pushing branches, creating pull requests, merging, rebasing, and force-pushing. These commands can alter repository state and require careful human review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands directly perform git and GitHub CLI operations. The skill also includes review and conflict guidance, so the evidence supports medium risk rather than high risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are Not Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown examples. The file is documentation, not executable Ruby code, although some documented shell commands are operationally sensitive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed content is Markdown fenced text and command examples. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution in an executable source file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Flags Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithms at the flagged lines, and the checklist monitor step is not network reconnaissance. These static hits appear to be false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows a skill description, a checklist item, and a git commit command. None contain cryptographic primitives or network scanning behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Non-Interactive Bash Execution Allowlist
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends allowlisting Bash git commands for non-interactive agent execution. Users should limit these permissions to known worktrees and review all diffs before merging.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission allowlist is directly shown in the documentation. The risk depends on user configuration and agent behavior, so confidence is high but not absolute.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Repository Mutation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include push, pull request creation, merge, rebase, and force-push commands. These are normal developer operations but can cause repository damage if misused.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and include remote-changing operations. The skill frames them as manual completion and conflict handling, which lowers maliciousness but not operational risk.

content.value_statement: Large features often stall when related issues block each other or overload one agent. This skill gives a practical workflow for planning waves, spawning agents, monitoring progress, and merging reviewed changes.
actual_capabilities: Breaks a large feature into small GitHub issues with clear file scope.
Organizes related issues into independent and dependent execution waves.
Shows how to spawn multiple Claude agents with non-interactive output logs.
Provides monitoring steps for running agents, worktrees, and log files.
Documents manual git and GitHub CLI steps for pull requests and cleanup.
Gives conflict-handling guidance for sequential merges and rebases.
limitations: It is a workflow guide and does not run agents by itself.
It assumes users understand git worktrees, branches, and pull requests.
It does not verify that generated agent changes are correct or secure.
It can increase coordination risk if too many agents modify related files.

use_cases.title: Split a Feature Across Issues
use_cases.description: Convert a broad engineering task into small issues with file ownership, acceptance criteria, and dependency order.

use_cases.title: Run Independent Coding Tasks
use_cases.description: Launch multiple agents for isolated backend, frontend, or test changes while keeping logs and worktrees separate.

use_cases.title: Merge Agent Pull Requests Safely
use_cases.description: Review agent results, handle conflicts, rebase affected branches, and clean up worktrees after merge.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a First Wave
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to split my feature request into three independent GitHub issues that one agent can complete each.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Agent Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft issue prompts with problem, solution, files to modify, implementation notes, and acceptance criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Parallel Execution
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to organize these issue numbers into waves, then give me a monitored execution plan for parallel agents.

prompt_templates.title: Recover and Merge Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect agent outcomes, identify stragglers, sequence merges, resolve conflict risk, and plan worktree cleanup.

output_examples.input: Plan parallel work for five related GitHub issues.
output: Wave 1 contains independent issues with separate files.
Wave 2 contains dependent or shared-file work after Wave 1 merges.
Each issue gets a log file, a worktree, and a review checkpoint.

output_examples.input: Help me recover after one agent finished code but did not create a pull request.
output: The response identifies the affected worktree and branch.
It lists manual review, commit, push, pull request, and cleanup steps.
It warns to inspect the diff before publishing remote changes.

output_examples.input: I have conflicts after merging earlier agent work.
output: The response recommends sequential conflict resolution.
It separates rebase, review, test, and force-push decisions.
It keeps same-file changes out of later parallel waves.

best_practices: Keep each issue focused on one to three files whenever possible.
Review every agent diff before committing, pushing, or merging changes.
Merge shared-file work sequentially and rebase between related pull requests.
anti_patterns: Do not let multiple agents edit the same file in the same wave.
Do not grant broad Bash permissions outside the intended worktree.
Do not merge agent pull requests without tests and human review.

faq.question: Does this skill create git worktrees automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and command examples for users or agents to execute.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: It is written for Claude workflows and is also relevant to Codex and Claude Code users.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with review gates. The workflow includes commands that can push and merge repository changes.

faq.question: What issue size works best?
faq.answer: Small issues work best, especially tasks limited to one to three files with clear acceptance criteria.

faq.question: How should I handle merge conflicts?
faq.answer: Put shared-file work in later waves or one agent, then merge sequentially and rebase after each related merge.

faq.question: Does it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly expects users to review diffs, resolve conflicts, and reject off-scope work.
